findings.detail: From: security@paypa1-accounts.com | Reply-To: harvest@phish-collect.ru
findings.explanation: The sender address and the Reply-To address differ. Replies go to a different person than the apparent sender — a classic phishing technique.

findings.detail: SPF authentication failed.
findings.explanation: This email was sent from a server not authorized for the claimed domain. Someone is impersonating this sender.

findings.detail: DKIM signature verification failed.
findings.explanation: The email's cryptographic signature is invalid — it may have been forged or tampered with.

findings.detail: DMARC policy check failed.
findings.explanation: Strong indicator this email is not from who it claims to be from.

findings.detail: Domain 'paypa1-secure.account-verify.ru' uses the TLD '.ru', commonly associated with phishing and spam.
findings.explanation: The '.ru' top-level domain is frequently used by attackers because it is cheap or free to register. Legitimate government and business websites rarely use this TLD.

findings.detail: Domain 'paypa1-secure.account-verify.ru' contains suspicious keyword(s): pay, verify, secure, account
findings.explanation: Attackers register domains containing words like 'fine', 'pay', 'toll', or agency names to impersonate government or payment sites. Legitimate agencies use official .gov domains.

findings.detail: URL uses a shortening service that hides the real destination.
findings.explanation: Attackers use URL shorteners to hide malicious links. The real destination is unknown until clicked.

findings.detail: Found 7 urgency/manipulation phrases: immediately, unusual activity, your account will be, unauthorized access, dear customer, suspension...
findings.explanation: This message uses multiple pressure tactics designed to make you act without thinking — a hallmark of phishing and smishing attacks.

findings.detail: Session 'sess_attack_001' switched from United States (98.23.45.67) to The Netherlands (91.108.56.23) without logout.
findings.explanation: The same session token was used from two different countries — a strong indicator that an attacker in a different location replayed a stolen session cookie.

findings.detail: Session 'sess_attack_001' used 2 different user agents: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) Chrome/120.0; python-requests/2.28.0
findings.explanation: A real user's browser does not change mid-session. Different user agents on the same session token indicate the token is being used by a different device — likely an attacker.

findings.detail: Session 'sess_attack_001' used from 2 different IPs within 100 seconds.
findings.explanation: The same session was used from multiple IP addresses in an impossibly short time — consistent with an attacker immediately replaying a stolen session token.

findings.detail: Session 'sess_attack_001' had 4 auth events with avg interval of 25.0s.
findings.explanation: Unusually frequent authentication or token refresh events can indicate automated tooling (like Evilginx2) relaying requests in real time.

findings.detail: Session 'sess_attack_002' used 2 different user agents: Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; CPU iPhone OS 16_0) Safari/604.1; curl/7.88.1
findings.explanation: A real user's browser does not change mid-session. Different user agents on the same session token indicate the token is being used by a different device — likely an attacker.

findings.detail: Session 'sess_attack_002' used from 2 different IPs within 75 seconds.
findings.explanation: The same session was used from multiple IP addresses in an impossibly short time — consistent with an attacker immediately replaying a stolen session token.

findings.detail: Session 'sess_attack_002' was active simultaneously from 185.220.101.45 and 192.168.1.10.
findings.explanation: Two different IP addresses were using the same session token at the same time — one is the legitimate user, the other is likely an attacker who stole the session cookie.

findings.explanation: The '.ru' top-level domain is frequently used by attackers because it is cheap or free to register. Legitimate government and business websites rarely use this TLD.

findings.detail: Domain 'paypa1-secure-login.verify-account.ru' contains suspicious keyword(s): pay, verify, secure, login, account
findings.explanation: Attackers register domains containing words like 'fine', 'pay', 'toll', or agency names to impersonate government or payment sites. Legitimate agencies use official .gov domains.

input_preview: NEW at KFC! Try the Crispy Shawarma Wrap at Rs.169. Loaded with chicken, fresh veggies and Lebanese sauce. Don't miss this! bit.ly/kfcind-shg21 TnC

findings.detail: Found 23 urgency/manipulation phrases: outstanding, traffic ticket, violation, suspension, driver's license, vehicle registration...
findings.explanation: This message uses multiple pressure tactics designed to make you act without thinking — a hallmark of phishing and smishing attacks.

findings.detail: Message uses legal threats: legal proceedings, credit damage
findings.explanation: Scammers use fake legal threats to create fear and urgency. Government agencies do not issue legal threats via SMS with payment links.

findings.detail: Message demands urgent payment with penalty threats: pay promptly, outstanding, failure to pay, processing charge
findings.explanation: This matches a known SMS smishing pattern where attackers impersonate government agencies to collect fake fines or tolls. Real agencies send official paper notices, not SMS links.

findings.detail: Message instructs user to 'Reply Y', reopen the message, or paste a link manually.
findings.explanation: This is a classic smishing evasion technique — instructing users to interact in ways that bypass link-click detection on mobile devices.

findings.detail: Message cites a specific legal code or statute to appear official.
findings.explanation: Scammers cite fake or real legal codes to appear authoritative. Legitimate government notices arrive by certified mail, not SMS.

findings.detail: Message impersonates government agency: illinois dot, idot, dmv, ssa, dot
findings.explanation: This message claims to be from a government agency. Legitimate agencies contact you via official mail or official .gov websites — never via SMS with payment links.

findings.detail: Domain 'idot.fine-ilwt.com' contains suspicious keyword(s): fine, ilwt, -il, idot
findings.explanation: Attackers register domains containing words like 'fine', 'pay', 'toll', or agency names to impersonate government or payment sites. Legitimate agencies use official .gov domains.

findings.detail: Domain 'idot.fine-ilwt.com' uses government-sounding keywords but is NOT a .gov domain.
findings.explanation: All legitimate US government websites use .gov domains. A site claiming to be a government agency on any other domain (like .com, .net, or others) is almost certainly fraudulent.

findings.detail: 'fine-ilwt.com' was registered 65 days ago.
findings.explanation: This domain is relatively new (65 days old), which can indicate it was created recently for malicious purposes.
